elements.sent-2411-001: This paper analyzes the motion of solutions to non-homogeneous linear differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-002: It further clarifies that a proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controller essentially comprises two parts: a homogeneous controller and a disturbance observer, which are responsible for stabilizing the homogeneous system and compensating for the lumped disturbances (non-homogeneous components) of the system respectively.
elements.sent-2411-003: Based on this framework, the impact of measurement noise on control performance is examined, and a parameter tuning scheme for the traditional PID controller is provided.
elements.sent-2411-004: Finally, as examples, controllers are designed for two representative control problems: a trajectory tracking controller for an underactuated vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL) aircraft in the time domain, and a lateral controller for a vehicle in the distance domain.
elements.sent-2411-005: Since its birth in the early 20th century, PID controller \cite{pid1 has been widely used in various engineering fields \cite{1453566 due to its simple structure, robustness and model-free characteristics.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: With the rapid development of linear algebra, increasing emphasis on Lyapunov stability analysis, and the rise of optimal control theory, various model-based control schemes—including adaptive control, sliding mode control, and optimal control methods such as linear quadratic regulator (LQR) and model predictive control (MPC)—have continually been developed.
elements.sent-2411-007: Despite the steady stream of advancements in control theory over the past half-century, PID control remains, to this day, the most widely used approach in engineering applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, these schemes often rely heavily on precise modeling, have a greater number of tuning parameters, or require more computational power.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, many new control algorithms are structurally complex, making it challenging for engineering practitioners to thoroughly understand their principles and apply them effectively in real production tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: In contrast, a simple PID control remains more comprehensible and easier to implement in today's engineering practices.
elements.sent-2411-011: PID control requires a set of effective parameters to enable the entire system to achieve the desired performance.
elements.sent-2411-012: Consequently, empirical methods for parameter tuning, such as the Ziegler-Nichols method \cite{znopen,znclose and relay tuning method \cite{ASTROM1984645 , have been developed.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, implementing these empirical approaches can be inefficient and complex, and the tuned parameters may still leave room for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-014: Additionally, parameter tuning methods based on linear system models have been designed \cite{772161,Borase2021,SOMEFUN202165 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Yet, due to model uncertainties, inaccuracies, unknown dynamics, and disturbances, it is challenging to analyze the system accurately in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, these methods have not gained sufficient attention or widespread application in PID control practices in engineering.
elements.sent-2411-017: In classical control theory, a tracking ability of system for different commands is qualitatively determined by ``type'' of the open-loop system.
elements.sent-2411-018: Influenced by this mindset, the integral component of PID controller is often interpreted individually and qualitatively as canceling steady-state error.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, is this truly the case, especially during parameter tuning? Inspired by the active disturbance rejection control (ADRC) paradigm \cite{4796887 , this paper reinterprets PID control from the perspective of state feedback for the error system and compensation for lumped disturbances, and presents a tuning method based on this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The aim is to provide engineering control practitioners with a new perspective for understanding, designing, and tuning controllers.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: Section 2 analyzes the motion of the solution to a non-homogeneous linear differential equation and presents the relationship between the final range of motion of the solution and the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-023: Section 3 reinterprets PID control from the perspective of state feedback and lumped disturbance compensation, providing a parameter tuning scheme.
elements.sent-2411-024: Section 4 illustrates the PID controller design process using two common systems as examples.
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, Section 5 presents a summary of the paper.
elements.section-2411-002: Motion of the Solution to a Non-Homogeneous Linear Differential Equation
elements.sent-2411-026: To make the analysis more general, consider a non-homogeneous linear differential equation as follows: \begin{equation}x^{(n)}+\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}a_ix^{(i)}=f\label{ODE}\end{equation} Here, $x$ is the state variable, $\bullet^{(n) =\frac{\mathrm{d ^n\bullet {\mathrm{d t^n}$, and $f=f(x,\cdots,x^{(n-1) ,t)$ represents the unknown dynamics.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The constants $a_i,i=0,1,\cdots, n-1$ satisfy $s^n+\sum_{i=0 ^{n-1 a_is^{i =\prod_{i=1 ^n(s+p_i)$, where this is a Hurwitz polynomial with real parts of $\mathrm{Re (p_i)>0$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Taking the Laplace transform of the above equation under zero initial conditions yields: \begin{equation}X(s)=\frac{1}{s^n+\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}a_is^{i}}F(s)\end{equation} where $X(s)=\mathcal{L [x(t)],F(s)=\mathcal{L [f(x(t),t)]$, $\mathcal{L}$ denotes the Laplace transform.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Clearly, when the unknown dynamics $f$ are bounded, the state $x$ is also bounded.
elements.sent-2411-030: To make the analysis more concise and clear, we now provide the solution for a special case.
elements.sent-2411-031: Consider the characteristic polynomial of the system having a repeated root, i.e. $p_i=\omega\in \mathbb{R ^+$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The differential equation can then be rewritten as \begin{equation}x^{(n)}=-\sum_{i=1}^nC_n^i\omega^ix^{(n-i)}+f\end{equation} where $C_n^i=\frac{n! {(n-i)!i!}$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this case, the solution to the equation is \begin{equation}x={} x_n+x_f\end{equation} where $x_n$ is the general solution of the homogeneous differential equation ($f=0$, corresponding to the zero-input response of the system), and $x_f$ is a particular solution of the non-homogeneous equation with zero initial conditions (corresponding to the zero-state response of the system).
elements.sent-2411-034: Their specific forms are as follows: \begin{equation}x_n={} e^{-\omega (t-t_0)}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\frac{t^i}{i!}\sum_{j=0}^iC_i^j\omega^{i-j}x^{(j)}(0) x_f={} \int_{t_0}^t\frac{(t-\tau)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}f(\tau)e^{-\omega(t-\tau)}d\tau\end{equation} For simplicity, we have omitted the intermediate variables, i.e., $f(t)=f(x(t),\cdots,x^{(n-1) (t),t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Clearly, the zero-input response of the system is always convergent, and the final motion state of the solution depends solely on the zero-state response $x_f$.
elements.sent-2411-037: It is also evident that when $\bar f:=\limsup_{t\to+\infty |f(t)|$ is a small constant, the final absolute value of the state of the system is very small.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The transfer function of the system is: \begin{equation}G(s)=\frac{X(s)}{F(s)}=\frac{1}{(s+\omega)^n}\end{equation} It can be seen that the state variable $x$ is the $n$-th order low-pass filtered output of $f$, with an output gain of $\omega^{-n}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Therefore, the output depends on two factors:.
elements.sent-2411-040: The bandwidth $\omega$: the larger the bandwidth, the smaller the gain of the filter, and thus the smaller the output.
elements.sent-2411-041: The unknown quantity $f$: the smaller $f$ is, the smaller the output will be.
elements.sent-2411-042: The bandwidth is constrained by the physical properties of the plant and typically has an upper limit.
elements.sent-2411-043: To minimize the final value of the solution, the unknown dynamics $f$ should be made as small as possible.
elements.section-2411-003: State Feedback and Lumped Disturbance Compensation
elements.sent-2411-044: From the analysis in the previous section, the following conclusion is readily apparent: for a bounded-input bounded-output (BIBO) system (\ref{ODE ) with unknown dynamics, the ultimate convergence behavior depends on the unknown dynamics $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Therefore, in this section, we propose a scheme to compensate for the system’s unknown dynamics to achieve better control performance.
elements.sent-2411-046: For the state $x$, control input $u$, unknown lumped disturbance $f=f(x,\cdots,x^{(n-1) ,u,t)$ , and input coefficient $b\neq 0$, the non-autonomous system is as follows: \begin{equation}x^{(n)} = f+bu,n\inN^+\label{system}\end{equation} The internal model principle reveals that the key to achieving good control performance lies in compensating for the unknown lumped disturbance $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, $f$ includes uncertain signals such as unknown dynamics and disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-048: Clearly, it is nearly impossible to observe $f$ without error; therefore, a reasonably simple observer must be designed to appropriately compensate for the lumped disturbance.
elements.sent-2411-049: At the same time, we need to stabilize the homogeneous system ($f=0$).
elements.sent-2411-050: For this purpose, we divide the control input $u$ into two components, as follows: \begin{equation}u=\frac{1}{b}(u_x-\hat{f})\label{input}\end{equation} where $\hat{f}$ is used to estimate (observe) and compensate for the unknown lumped disturbance $f$ and $u_x$, referred to as the homogeneous controller, is used to drive the entire homogeneous system to convergence.
elements.sent-2411-051: Based on the analysis in the previous section, design \begin{equation}u_x=-\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}a_ix^{(i)}\end{equation} and define the observation error of the unknown dynamics as \begin{equation}\tilde{f}:=f-\hat{f}\label{obserror}\end{equation} Substituting Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-052: For simplicity, the term $\omega_fx^{(n-1) (0)$ is omitted here.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Since it can be considered part of the unknown lumped disturbance $f$, this omission does not affect the conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-054: The rationale for omitting initial values of integrals in subsequent analysis in this section is consistent with this explanation and will not be repeated.
elements.sent-2411-055: Taking the Laplace transform of the above equation under zero initial conditions, the transfer function of this observer is given by: \begin{equation}G_o(s)=\frac{\hat{F}(s)}{F(s)}=\frac{\omega_f}{s+\omega_f}\end{equation} Further, the observation error is given by: \begin{equation}\tilde{f}=f-\omega_f\int_0^t\tilde{f}dt\end{equation} It can also be seen that the error transfer function is: \begin{equation}G_e(s)=\frac{\tilde{F}(s)}{F(s)}=\frac{s}{s+\omega_f}\end{equation} It can be seen that the observer bandwidth $\omega_f$ affects the performance of the observer.
elements.sent-2411-056: The larger the bandwidth, the closer the transfer function of the observer is to 1, resulting in a smaller observation error, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-057: Furthermore, it follows that the transfer function of the entire system is: \begin{equation}G(s)=\frac{X(s)}{F(s)}=\frac{s}{(s^n+\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}a_is^{i})(s+\omega_f)}\end{equation} The impact of noise on control performance cannot be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-058: The effect of noise amplification due to the gains needs to be qualitatively analyzed in order to find the optimal control parameters.
elements.sent-2411-059: In engineering practice, it is nearly impossible to measure each state variable without error; therefore, the measured values are defined as follows: \begin{equation}z_i = x^{(i)}+w_i,i=0,1,\cdots, n-1\end{equation} where $w_i$ represents the measurement noise.
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore, during parameter tuning, the bandwidth should be carefully selected based on the noise frequency.
elements.sent-2411-062: A reasonable approach is to set the homogeneous controller bandwidth smaller than the observer bandwidth to minimize the impact of noise on the system performance.
elements.sent-2411-063: This is similar to the separation principle, where the parameters of the observer and the homogeneous controller can be designed separately without considering their coupling.
elements.sent-2411-064: We can simplistically assume that the reference of the homogeneous controller only determines the system’s poles, while the observer parameters only determine the observer’s bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this way, the loop shaping process of a system is decomposed into tuning two relatively low-order closed-loop systems.
elements.sent-2411-066: However, for the homogeneous controller, there are still multiple parameters that need to be tuned.
elements.sent-2411-067: This paper recommends configuring the poles of the homogeneous system to be the same value \cite{1242516 , that is : \begin{equation}u_x=-\sum_{i=1}^nC_n^i\omega^ix^{(n-i)}\end{equation} The benefits of this approach are evident: the entire homogeneous controller has only one parameter, $\omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Its physical significance, along with its reciprocal $T=\frac{1 {\omega}$, is also clear, representing the bandwidth and the time constant of the dynamic response.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In addition, to achieve better performance from the PID controller, it is best to know the input coefficient $b$ of the controller, even if only an approximate value.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the above analysis, we have essentially examined the functions of the various components of the ``generalized PID'' controller, namely, the state feedback component for stabilizing the homogeneous system and the observation component for compensating lumped disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-071: The reason for using ``generalized PID'' to describe the above controller is that it is merely a higher-order extension and generalization of the PID controller for second-order systems.
elements.sent-2411-072: By applying simple simplifications, one can derive the PI controller for first-order systems and the PID controller for second-order systems.
elements.sent-2411-073: To make the description clearer and in comparison with the PID, we will briefly outline this process below.
elements.sent-2411-074: First, we simplify the above system (\ref{system ) to a first-order system, which gives: \begin{equation}\dot{x}= f+bu\end{equation} Accordingly, the homogeneous controller and the lumped disturbance observer are: \begin{equation}u_x={} -a_0 x \hat f ={} -\omega_f\int_0^tu_xdt\end{equation} Substituting $u_x$ and $\hat{f}$ into the controller $u$, we get: \begin{equation}u={} \frac{1}{b}\left(-a_0 x-\omega_fa_0\int_0^t xdt\right)\end{equation} Clearly, the above equation represents a PI controller ($k_p=a_0, k_i=\omega_fa_0$).}
elements.sent-2411-075: Similarly, when system (\ref{system ) is instantiated as a second-order system, we have: \begin{equation}\ddot{x}= f+bu\end{equation} Accordingly, the homogeneous controller and the lumped disturbance observer are: \begin{equation}u_x={} -a_1\dot{x}-a_0 x \hat f ={} \omega_f\left(\dot{x} - \int_0^tu_xdt\right)\end{equation} Substituting $u_x$ and $\hat{f}$ into the controller $u$, we get: \begin{equation}u={} \frac{1}{b}\left(-a_1\dot{x}-a_0 x-\omega_f\left(\dot{x}- \int_0^t (-a_1\dot{x}-a_0 x)dt\right)\right)\notag ={} \frac{1}{b}\left(-(a_1+\omega_f)\dot{x}-(a_0+\omega_fa_1) x-\omega_fa_0\int_0^txdt\right)\end{equation} Clearly, the above equation represents a PID controller ($k_d=a_1+\omega_f,k_p=a_0+\omega_fa_1,k_i=\omega_fa_0$).}
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section, we will design controllers for two representative physical systems.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Trajectory Tracking Control for Underactuated VTOL Aircraft
elements.sent-2411-077: The underactuated VTOL aircraft, as a 6-degree-of-freedom (DOF) rigid body, can be used to describe the models of quadrotors, helicopters, and other aircraft.
elements.sent-2411-078: Its mathematical model is as follows: \begin{equation}\left\{\dot{\vp}={} \vv m\dot{\vv}={} mg\ve_3-f\mR \ve_3+\vd_f \dot{\mR}={} \mR \vo_\times \mJ\dot{\vo}={} -\vo\times (\mJ\vo)+\vt+\vd_\tau\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The subscript $\times$ describes the following mapping: \begin{equation}\left[ x_1 x_2 x_3 \right]_\times=\left[ 0 -x_3 x_2 x_3 0 -x_1 -x_2 x_1 0 \right]\end{equation} The control objective is to track the 3D trajectory $\vp_d\in\mathbb{R ^3$ and the flight orientation angle $\psi_d$.}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\left[ x_1 x_2 x_3 \right]_\times=\left[ 0 -x_3 x_2 x_3 0 -x_1 -x_2 x_1 0 \right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-080: By simplifying our previous work \cite{Sujie2024 , we obtain the error model: \begin{equation}\left\{\ddot{\tilde{\vp}}={} g\ve_3-\frac{f}{m}\mR\ve_3+\frac{1}{m}\vd_f-\ddot{\vp}_d \ddot{\tilde{\vg}}={} \mG(\mJ^{-1}(-\vo_\times \mJ\vo+\vt+\vd_\tau)+(\mG^{-1}\dot{\tilde{\vg}})_\times\vo-\tilde{\mR}^\top\dot{\vo}_d) +\frac{2\tilde{\vg}^\top\dot{\tilde{\vg}}\dot{\tilde{\vg}}}{1+\tilde{\vg}^\top\tilde{\vg}}\right.\label{vtolerrormodel}\end{equation} where $\mR_d=[\vb_{1d ,\vb_{2d , \vb_{3d ]=[\vb_{2d\times}\vb_{3d ,\frac{\vb_{3d\times}\vb_d {\Vert\vb_{3d\times}\vb_d\Vert ,\frac{\vF_d {\Vert \vF_d\Vert ]\in\mathrm{SO (3)$ describes the desired attitude with the desired control thrust $\vF_d\in\mathbb{R ^3$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The angular velocity error is described by $\tilde{\vo =\vo-\tilde{\mR ^\top\vo_d$, with the desired attitude angular velocity given by $\vo_{d\times ={\mR _d^\top\dot{\mR _d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The kinematic model of the vehicle is typically described by the bicycle model as follows: \begin{equation}\left\{\dot{x}={} v\cos\theta \dot{y}={} v\sin\theta \dot{\theta}={} \frac{v\tan(\delta+d)}{L}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[height=4.0cm]{vehicle.eps} \caption{Schematic diagram of vehicle lateral path tracking in Frenet--Serret frame} \label{figlattrack}
elements.sent-2411-084: For lateral control in path tracking problems, we do not focus on the speed of the vehicle information; therefore, we can convert the kinematics to the distance (arc-length) domain, as follows: \begin{equation}\left\{x'={} \cos\theta y'={} \sin\theta \theta'={} \frac{\tan(\delta+d)}{L}\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Where $\bullet'=\frac{\mathrm{d}\bullet {\mathrm{d s}$, and in this section, $s$ represents the distance traveled by the vehicle, with $\dot{s =v$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: As As shown in Figure \ref{figlattrack , for a given reference path $\mathcal{P :\mathbb{R ^+\to\mathbb{R ^2\times\mathbb{S}$, $\vp_d=[x_d,y_d,\theta_d]^\top$ is the matching point of the vehicle on $\mathcal{P}$ in the Frenet-Serret frame \cite{Frenet1852,Serret1851 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The error is defined as: \begin{equation}e_x=x_d-x,e_y=y_d-y,e_\theta=\theta_d-\theta\end{equation} According to the definition of the Frenet-Serret frame, the following constraints hold: \begin{equation}\left[ 0 l \right]=\left[ \cos\theta_d \sin\theta_d -\sin\theta_d \cos\theta_d \right]\left[ e_x e_y \right]= \left[ e_x\cos\theta_d+e_y\sin\theta_d -e_x\sin\theta_d+e_y\cos\theta_d \right]\label{latconstrains}\end{equation} where $l$ represents the lateral error.
elements.sent-2411-088: To facilitate the subsequent development of the error model, we assume that $e_\theta\in(-\frac{\pi {2 ,\frac{\pi {2 )$, and that for the path arc length $s_d$, $r_s:=\frac{\mathrm{d s_d {\mathrm{d s}$ exists.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: In fact, for $v\neq 0$ we have $r_s=\frac{v_d {v}$, where $v_d=\sqrt{\dot{x _d^2+\dot{y _d^2}$ is the velocity of the matching point, rather than the result of the velocity planning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Additionally, it can be noted that if the lateral control tracking error is very small, we can assume that $r_s\approx 1$.
elements.sent-2411-091: By substituting the above constraint equations into the derivative of $l$ with respect to $s$, we get: \begin{equation}l'={} -r_s\kappa_d(e_x\cos\theta_d +e_y\sin\theta_d)-\sin\theta_d(r_s\cos\theta_d-\cos\theta)\notag +\cos\theta_d(r_s\sin\theta_d-\sin\theta) ={} \sin(\theta_d-\theta)=\sin e_\theta \label{latfirstorder}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\left\{\frac{dx_d}{ds_d}={} \cos\theta_d \frac{dy_d}{ds_d}={} \sin\theta_d \frac{d\theta_d}{ds_d}={} \kappa_d\right. \Longrightarrow\ \left\{\frac{dx_d}{ds}={} r_s\cos\theta_d \frac{dy_d}{ds}={} r_s\sin\theta_d \frac{d\theta_d}{ds}={} r_s\kappa_d\right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Continuing with the differentiation, we get: \begin{equation}l''={} \cos e_\theta(r_s\kappa_d-\frac{\tan(\delta+d)}{L})\label{latsecondorder}\end{equation} where $\bullet''=\frac{\mathrm{d ^2\bullet {\mathrm{d s^2}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: If $d$ is known and assuming $r_s=1$, we can directly design the state feedback control law as follows: \begin{equation}\delta=\arctan\left(L(\kappa_d+\sec e_\theta(k_0l+k_1\sin e_\theta))\right)-d\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: As shown in Figure \ref{figlattrackvehicle , if the intersection of path $\mathcal{P}$ and the rear axle of the vehicle is taken as the projection point $\vp_d$, the lateral error is still defined as the distance between $\vp$ and $\vp_d$.}
elements.sent-2411-095: It is only necessary to transform the errors $e_x$ and $e_y$ into the vehicle coordinate system to obtain constraints similar to Eq. (\ref{latconstrains ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: By taking the first and second derivatives of $l$ with respect to $s$, the model for the lateral error can be derived.
elements.sent-2411-097: The specific process is not elaborated further in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-098: It is worth noting that although the selection scheme for matching may differ, the lateral error model is exactly the same as the one in equations (\ref{latfirstorder ) and (\ref{latsecondorder ).}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[height=4.0cm]{vehicle1.eps} \caption{Schematic diagram of vehicle lateral path tracking in vehicle frame} \label{figlattrackvehicle}
elements.sent-2411-099: When the disturbance is unknown, the error model is transformed as follows: \begin{equation}l''={} -\frac{\cos e_\theta\tan\delta}{L}+d^{lump} d^{lump}={} \cos e_\theta\left(r_s\kappa_d-\frac{\tan d(1+\tan^2\delta)}{L(1-\tan\delta\tan d)}\right)\notag\end{equation} The controller is designed as follows: \begin{equation}\left\{\delta =\arctan(L\sec e_\theta(u_x+\hat{d}^lump)) u_x =k_0l+k_1l'=k_0l+k_1\sin e_\theta \hat{d}^lump =\omega_d(l'+\int_0^su_xds)=\omega_d(\sin e_\theta+\int_0^tvu_xdt)\right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-100: This paper reinterprets the principle of PID controllers by drawing on the motion of the solution to non-homogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-101: The PID controller consists of two components: the state feedback of the homogeneous system and the compensation of lumped disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-102: Based on this, we present a parameter tuning scheme for PID controllers.
elements.sent-2411-103: For the homogeneous system, appropriate pole placement is sufficient to achieve the desired convergence behavior.
elements.sent-2411-104: As for the compensation of lumped disturbances, we only need to adjust the bandwidth of the observer.
elements.sent-2411-105: Although the scheme for compensating lumped disturbances in PID is simple, it is not always the most suitable.
elements.sent-2411-106: Fortunately, we now clearly understand that the controller essentially consists of two parts: the homogeneous system controller and the lumped disturbance observer.
elements.sent-2411-107: Therefore, we can choose different schemes for observing and compensating lumped disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this context, we recommend the ADRC paradigm to engineers, as it is the main inspiration for this paper.
elements.sent-2411-109: Below, we outline the advantages of Extended State Observer (ESO \cite{4796887 , including linear ESO (LESO) \cite{1242516 and generalized ESO (GESO) \cite{6117083 ) in the ADRC control paradigm:.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The ESO requires minimal prior knowledge of the system, needing only the system order, input coefficients, and output signals.
elements.sent-2411-111: Not all state variables of a system are always available, and numerically differentiating the output to approximate derivatives often leads to the amplification of noise.
elements.sent-2411-112: The ESO, being an output feedback-based observer, can observe both lumped disturbances and all the state variables of the system.
elements.sent-2411-113: For multi-input, multi-output systems with mismatched disturbances, it is difficult to estimate the disturbances using conventional methods.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, the GESO provides a feasible solution.
elements.sent-2411-115: The parameters of the ESO typically only determine the performance of the observer.
elements.sent-2411-116: We can independently assign zeros and poles to both the observer and the controller, which means that the separation principle remains applicable in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-117: Finally, for two common control problems—the trajectory tracking of an underactuated VTOL aircraft and the lateral control of a vehicle—we have designed PID controllers based on their error models.
elements.sent-2411-118: Here, we summarize the control design process and present a more general set of design steps:.
elements.sent-2411-119: Deriving the Mathematical Model of the Error in the Control Problem: Ideally, the error model should be in the form of an integral cascade structure, as this simplifies both controller and observer design.
elements.sent-2411-120: For this purpose, input-output feedback linearization \cite{khalil2002nonlinear is often a useful theoretical tool.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, for certain control tasks, it may be beneficial to break free from traditional thinking and build models in alternative dimensions, as demonstrated in Section \ref{sebsectionLCV , where the controller is designed in the distance domain.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Decomposing the Error Model into Input Signals and Lumped Disturbances: It is important to separate the error system into these two components for a more structured approach to control design.
elements.sent-2411-123: Selecting a Control Scheme and Designing the Controller: If the mathematical model of the error system is simple and all relevant information is known, one mFrenetay directly design the control law without the need for integrators or observers.
elements.sent-2411-124: For more complex cases, the PID controller introduced in this paper or the ADRC paradigm can be applied.
